[1m[7m%[27m[1m[0m                                                                                                                                                                                                          ]7;file://mindstix-ka-MacBook-Air.local/Users/sridharan/Desktop/scripts[0m[27m[24m[Jsridharan@mindstix-ka-MacBook-Air scripts % [K[?2004hcclear[?2004l

[?1049h[63;1H[?1h=TCPDUMP(1)                                                                               General Commands Manual                                                                              TCPDUMP(1)

[1mNAME[0m
       tcpdump - dump traffic on a network

[1mSYNOPSIS[0m
       [1mtcpdump[0m [ [1m-AbdDefhHIJKlLnNOpqStuUvxX#[0m ] [ [1m-B[0m [4mbuffer_size[24m ]
               [ [1m-c[0m [4mcount[24m ] [ [1m--count[0m ] [ [1m-C[0m [4mfile_size[24m ]
               [ [1m-E[0m [4mspi@ipaddr[24m [4malgo:secret,...[24m  ]
               [ [1m-F[0m [4mfile[24m ] [ [1m-G[0m [4mrotate_seconds[24m ] [ [1m-i[0m [4minterface[24m ]
               [ [1m--immediate-mode[0m ] [ [1m-j[0m [4mtstamp_type[24m ] [ [1m-k[0m [4m(metadata_arg)[24m ]
               [ [1m-m[0m [4mmodule[24m ]
               [ [1m-M[0m [4msecret[24m ] [ [1m--number[0m ] [ [1m--print[0m ]
               [ [1m-Q[0m [4mpacket-metadata-filter[24m ] [ [1m-Q[0m [4min|out|inout[24m ]
               [ [1m-r[0m [4mfile[24m ] [ [1m-s[0m [4msnaplen[24m ] [ [1m-T[0m [4mtype[24m ] [ [1m--version[0m ]
               [ [1m-V[0m [4mfile[24m ] [ [1m-w[0m [4mfile[24m ] [ [1m-W[0m [4mfilecount[24m ] [ [1m-y[0m [4mdatalinktype[24m ]
               [ [1m-z[0m [4mpostrotate-command[24m ] [ [1m-Z[0m [4muser[24m ]
               [ [1m--time-stamp-precision=[0m[4mtstamp_precision[24m ]
               [ [1m--micro[0m ] [ [1m--nano[0m ]
               [ [4mexpression[24m ]

[1mDESCRIPTION[0m
       [4mTcpdump[24m prints out a description of the contents of packets on a network interface that match the Boolean [4mexpression[24m; the description is preceded by a time stamp, printed, by default, as hours,
       minutes, seconds, and fractions of a second since midnight.  It can also be run with the [1m-w[0m flag, which causes it to save the packet data to a file for later analysis, and/or with the [1m-r[0m flag,
       which causes it to read from a saved packet file rather than to read packets from a network interface.  It can also be run with the [1m-V[0m flag, which causes it to read a list of saved packet
       files. In all cases, only packets that match [4mexpression[24m will be processed by [4mtcpdump[24m.

       [4mTcpdump[24m will, if not run with the [1m-c[0m flag, continue capturing packets until it is interrupted by a SIGINT signal (generated, for example, by typing your interrupt character, typically control-
       C) or a SIGTERM signal (typically generated with the [1mkill[0m(1) command); if run with the [1m-c[0m flag, it will capture packets until it is interrupted by a SIGINT or SIGTERM signal or the specified
       number of packets have been processed.

       When [4mtcpdump[24m finishes capturing packets, it will report counts of:

              packets ``captured'' (this is the number of packets that [4mtcpdump[24m has received and processed);

              packets ``received by filter'' (the meaning of this depends on the OS on which you're running [4mtcpdump[24m, and possibly on the way the OS was configured - if a filter was specified on the
              command line, on some OSes it counts packets regardless of whether they were matched by the filter expression and, even if they were matched by the filter expression, regardless of
              whether [4mtcpdump[24m has read and processed them yet, on other OSes it counts only packets that were matched by the filter expression regardless of whether [4mtcpdump[24m has read and processed them
              yet, and on other OSes it counts only packets that were matched by the filter expression and were processed by [4mtcpdump[24m);

              packets ``dropped by kernel'' (this is the number of packets that were dropped, due to a lack of buffer space, by the packet capture mechanism in the OS on which [4mtcpdump[24m is running, if
              the OS reports that information to applications; if not, it will be reported as 0).

       On platforms that support the SIGINFO signal, such as most BSDs (including macOS) and Digital/Tru64 UNIX, it will report those counts when it receives a SIGINFO signal (generated, for example,
       by typing your ``status'' character, typically control-T, although on some platforms, such as macOS, the ``status'' character is not set by default, so you must set it with [1mstty[0m(1) in order to
       use it) and will continue capturing packets. On platforms that do not support the SIGINFO signal, the same can be achieved by using the SIGUSR1 signal.

       Using the SIGUSR2 signal along with the [1m-w[0m flag will forcibly flush the packet buffer into the output file.

       Reading packets from a network interface may require that you have special privileges; see the [1mpcap[0m(3PCAP) man page for details.  Reading a saved packet file doesn't require special privileges.

[1mOPTIONS[0m
       [1m-A[0m     Print each packet (minus its link level header) in ASCII.  Handy for capturing web pages.

       [1m-b[0m     Print the AS number in BGP packets in ASDOT notation rather than ASPLAIN notation.

       [1m-B[0m [4mbuffer_size[24m
       [1m--buffer-size=[0m[4mbuffer_size[24m
              Set the operating system capture buffer size to [4mbuffer_size[24m, in units of KiB (1024 bytes).

       [1m-c[0m [4mcount[24m
              Exit after receiving [4mcount[24m packets.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-c[0m [4mskip,count[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Exit after receiving or displaying [4mcount[24m packets.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              The second form allows to pass the number of initial packets to ignore with the [1mskip[0m parameter. The [1mskip[0m parameter is required before the comma but the [1mcount[0m parameters is optional after
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              the comma.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--count[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Print only on stderr the packet count when reading capture file(s) instead of parsing/printing the packets. If a filter is specified on the command line, [4mtcpdump[24m counts only packets that
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              were matched by the filter expression.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-C[0m [4mfile_size[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Before writing a raw packet to a savefile, check whether the file is currently larger than [4mfile_size[24m and, if so, close the current savefile and open a new one.  Savefiles after the first
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              savefile will have the name specified with the [1m-w[0m flag, with a number after it, starting at 1 and continuing upward.  The units of [4mfile_size[24m are millions of bytes (1,000,000 bytes, not
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              1,048,576 bytes).
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-d[0m     Dump the compiled packet-matching code in a human readable form to standard output and stop.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Please mind that although code compilation is always DLT-specific, typically it is impossible (and unnecessary) to specify which DLT to use for the dump because [4mtcpdump[24m uses either the
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              DLT of the input pcap file specified with [1m-r[0m, or the default DLT of the network interface specified with [1m-i[0m, or the particular DLT of the network interface specified with [1m-y[0m and [1m-i[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              respectively. In these cases the dump shows the same exact code that would filter the input file or the network interface without [1m-d[0m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              However, when neither [1m-r[0m nor [1m-i[0m is specified, specifying [1m-d[0m prevents [4mtcpdump[24m from guessing a suitable network interface (see [1m-i[0m).  In this case the DLT defaults to EN10MB and can be set
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              to another valid value manually with [1m-y[0m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-dd[0m    Dump packet-matching code as a [1mC[0m program fragment.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-ddd[0m   Dump packet-matching code as decimal numbers (preceded with a count).
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-D[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--list-interfaces[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Print the list of the network interfaces available on the system and on which [4mtcpdump[24m can capture packets.  For each network interface, a number and an interface name, possibly followed
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              by a text description of the interface, are printed.  The interface name or the number can be supplied to the [1m-i[0m flag to specify an interface on which to capture.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              This can be useful on systems that don't have a command to list them (e.g., Windows systems, or UNIX systems lacking [1mifconfig[0m [1m-a[0m); the number can be useful on Windows 2000 and later
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              systems, where the interface name is a somewhat complex string.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              The [1m-D[0m flag will not be supported if [4mtcpdump[24m was built with an older version of [4mlibpcap[24m that lacks the [1mpcap_findalldevs(3PCAP)[0m function.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-e[0m     Print the link-level header on each dump line.  This can be used, for example, to print MAC layer addresses for protocols such as Ethernet and IEEE 802.11.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              yet, and on other OSes it counts only packets that were matched by the filter expression and were processed by [4mtcpdump[24m);
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              whether [4mtcpdump[24m has read and processed them yet, on other OSes it counts only packets that were matched by the filter expression regardless of whether [4mtcpdump[24m has read and processed them
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              command line, on some OSes it counts packets regardless of whether they were matched by the filter expression and, even if they were matched by the filter expression, regardless of
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              packets ``received by filter'' (the meaning of this depends on the OS on which you're running [4mtcpdump[24m, and possibly on the way the OS was configured - if a filter was specified on the
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              packets ``captured'' (this is the number of packets that [4mtcpdump[24m has received and processed);
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       When [4mtcpdump[24m finishes capturing packets, it will report counts of:
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       number of packets have been processed.
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       C) or a SIGTERM signal (typically generated with the [1mkill[0m(1) command); if run with the [1m-c[0m flag, it will capture packets until it is interrupted by a SIGINT or SIGTERM signal or the specified
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       [4mTcpdump[24m will, if not run with the [1m-c[0m flag, continue capturing packets until it is interrupted by a SIGINT signal (generated, for example, by typing your interrupt character, typically control-
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       files. In all cases, only packets that match [4mexpression[24m will be processed by [4mtcpdump[24m.
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       which causes it to read from a saved packet file rather than to read packets from a network interface.  It can also be run with the [1m-V[0m flag, which causes it to read a list of saved packet
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       minutes, seconds, and fractions of a second since midnight.  It can also be run with the [1m-w[0m flag, which causes it to save the packet data to a file for later analysis, and/or with the [1m-r[0m flag,
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       [4mTcpdump[24m prints out a description of the contents of packets on a network interface that match the Boolean [4mexpression[24m; the description is preceded by a time stamp, printed, by default, as hours,
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM[1mDESCRIPTION[0m
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM               [ [4mexpression[24m ]

[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM               [ [1m-z[0m [4mpostrotate-command[24m ] [ [1m-Z[0m [4muser[24m ]
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM               [ [1m-V[0m [4mfile[24m ] [ [1m-w[0m [4mfile[24m ] [ [1m-W[0m [4mfilecount[24m ] [ [1m-y[0m [4mdatalinktype[24m ]

:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              by a text description of the interface, are printed.  The interface name or the number can be supplied to the [1m-i[0m flag to specify an interface on which to capture.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              This can be useful on systems that don't have a command to list them (e.g., Windows systems, or UNIX systems lacking [1mifconfig[0m [1m-a[0m); the number can be useful on Windows 2000 and later
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              systems, where the interface name is a somewhat complex string.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              The [1m-D[0m flag will not be supported if [4mtcpdump[24m was built with an older version of [4mlibpcap[24m that lacks the [1mpcap_findalldevs(3PCAP)[0m function.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-e[0m     Print the link-level header on each dump line.  This can be used, for example, to print MAC layer addresses for protocols such as Ethernet and IEEE 802.11.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-E[0m     Use [4mspi@ipaddr[24m [4malgo:secret[24m for decrypting IPsec ESP packets that are addressed to [4maddr[24m and contain Security Parameter Index value [4mspi[24m. This combination may be repeated with comma or
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              newline separation.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Note that setting the secret for IPv4 ESP packets is supported at this time.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Algorithms may be [1mdes-cbc[0m, [1m3des-cbc[0m, [1mblowfish-cbc[0m, [1mrc3-cbc[0m, [1mcast128-cbc[0m, or [1mnone[0m.  The default is [1mdes-cbc[0m.  The ability to decrypt packets is only present if [4mtcpdump[24m was compiled with
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              cryptography enabled.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              [4msecret[24m is the ASCII text for ESP secret key.  If preceded by 0x, then a hex value will be read.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              The option assumes RFC2406 ESP, not RFC1827 ESP.  The option is only for debugging purposes, and the use of this option with a true `secret' key is discouraged.  By presenting IPsec
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              secret key onto command line you make it visible to others, via [4mps[24m(1) and other occasions.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              In addition to the above syntax, the syntax [4mfile[24m [4mname[24m may be used to have tcpdump read the provided file in. The file is opened upon receiving the first ESP packet, so any special
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              permissions that tcpdump may have been given should already have been given up.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-f[0m     Print `foreign' IPv4 addresses numerically rather than symbolically (this option is intended to get around serious brain damage in Sun's NIS server — usually it hangs forever translating
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              non-local internet numbers).
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              The test for `foreign' IPv4 addresses is done using the IPv4 address and netmask of the interface on which capture is being done.  If that address or netmask are not available,
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              available, either because the interface on which capture is being done has no address or netmask or because the capture is being done on the Linux "any" interface, which can capture on
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              more than one interface, this option will not work correctly.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-F[0m [4mfile[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Use [4mfile[24m as input for the filter expression.  An additional expression given on the command line is ignored.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-g[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--apple-oneline[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Do not insert line break after IP header in verbose mode for easier parsing. This is an Apple addition.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-G[0m [4mrotate_seconds[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If specified, rotates the dump file specified with the [1m-w[0m option every [4mrotate_seconds[24m seconds.  Savefiles will have the name specified by [1m-w[0m which should include a time format as defined
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              by [1mstrftime[0m(3).  If no time format is specified, each new file will overwrite the previous.  Whenever a generated filename is not unique, tcpdump will overwrite the pre-existing data;
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              providing a time specification that is coarser than the capture period is therefore not advised.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If used in conjunction with the [1m-C[0m option, filenames will take the form of `[4mfile[24m<count>'.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-h[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--help[0m Print the tcpdump and libpcap version strings, print a usage message, and exit.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--version[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Print the tcpdump and libpcap version strings and exit.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-H[0m     Attempt to detect 802.11s draft mesh headers.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-i[0m [4minterface[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--interface=[0m[4minterface[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Listen, report the list of link-layer types, report the list of time stamp types, or report the results of compiling a filter expression on [4minterface[24m.  If unspecified and if the [1m-d[0m flag
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              is not given, [4mtcpdump[24m searches the system interface list for the lowest numbered, configured up interface (excluding loopback), which may turn out to be, for example, ``eth0''.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              On Linux systems with 2.2 or later kernels, an [4minterface[24m argument of ``any'' can be used to capture packets from all interfaces.  Note that captures on the ``any'' device will not be
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              done in promiscuous mode.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              On Darwin systems version 13 or later, when the [4minterface[24m is unspecified, tcpdump will use a pseudo interface to capture packets on a set of interfaces determined by the kernel (excludes
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              by default loopback and tunnel interfaces).
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Alternatively, to capture on more than one interface at a time, one may use "pktap" as the [4minterface[24m parameter followed by an optional list of comma separated interface names to include.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              For example, to capture on the loopback and en0 interface:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K                     [1mtcpdump[0m [1m-i[0m [1mpktap,lo0,en0[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              An [4minterface[24m argument of "all" or "pktap,all" can be used to capture packets from all interfaces, including loopback and tunnel interfaces.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       [1m-e[0m     Print the link-level header on each dump line.  This can be used, for example, to print MAC layer addresses for protocols such as Ethernet and IEEE 802.11.
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              The [1m-D[0m flag will not be supported if [4mtcpdump[24m was built with an older version of [4mlibpcap[24m that lacks the [1mpcap_findalldevs(3PCAP)[0m function.
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              systems, where the interface name is a somewhat complex string.
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              This can be useful on systems that don't have a command to list them (e.g., Windows systems, or UNIX systems lacking [1mifconfig[0m [1m-a[0m); the number can be useful on Windows 2000 and later
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              by a text description of the interface, are printed.  The interface name or the number can be supplied to the [1m-i[0m flag to specify an interface on which to capture.
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              Print the list of the network interfaces available on the system and on which [4mtcpdump[24m can capture packets.  For each network interface, a number and an interface name, possibly followed
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       [1m--list-interfaces[0m
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       [1m-D[0m
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       [1m-ddd[0m   Dump packet-matching code as decimal numbers (preceded with a count).
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       [1m-dd[0m    Dump packet-matching code as a [1mC[0m program fragment.
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              to another valid value manually with [1m-y[0m.
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              However, when neither [1m-r[0m nor [1m-i[0m is specified, specifying [1m-d[0m prevents [4mtcpdump[24m from guessing a suitable network interface (see [1m-i[0m).  In this case the DLT defaults to EN10MB and can be set
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              respectively. In these cases the dump shows the same exact code that would filter the input file or the network interface without [1m-d[0m.
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              DLT of the input pcap file specified with [1m-r[0m, or the default DLT of the network interface specified with [1m-i[0m, or the particular DLT of the network interface specified with [1m-y[0m and [1m-i[0m
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              Please mind that although code compilation is always DLT-specific, typically it is impossible (and unnecessary) to specify which DLT to use for the dump because [4mtcpdump[24m uses either the
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       [1m-d[0m     Dump the compiled packet-matching code in a human readable form to standard output and stop.
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              1,048,576 bytes).
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              savefile will have the name specified with the [1m-w[0m flag, with a number after it, starting at 1 and continuing upward.  The units of [4mfile_size[24m are millions of bytes (1,000,000 bytes, not
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              Before writing a raw packet to a savefile, check whether the file is currently larger than [4mfile_size[24m and, if so, close the current savefile and open a new one.  Savefiles after the first
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       [1m-C[0m [4mfile_size[24m
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              were matched by the filter expression.
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              Print only on stderr the packet count when reading capture file(s) instead of parsing/printing the packets. If a filter is specified on the command line, [4mtcpdump[24m counts only packets that
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       [1m--count[0m
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              the comma.
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              The second form allows to pass the number of initial packets to ignore with the [1mskip[0m parameter. The [1mskip[0m parameter is required before the comma but the [1mcount[0m parameters is optional after
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              Exit after receiving or displaying [4mcount[24m packets.
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       [1m-c[0m [4mskip,count[24m
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              Exit after receiving [4mcount[24m packets.
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       [1m-c[0m [4mcount[24m
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              Set the operating system capture buffer size to [4mbuffer_size[24m, in units of KiB (1024 bytes).
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       [1m--buffer-size=[0m[4mbuffer_size[24m
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       [1m-B[0m [4mbuffer_size[24m
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       [1m-b[0m     Print the AS number in BGP packets in ASDOT notation rather than ASPLAIN notation.
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       [1m-A[0m     Print each packet (minus its link level header) in ASCII.  Handy for capturing web pages.
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM[1mOPTIONS[0m
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       Reading packets from a network interface may require that you have special privileges; see the [1mpcap[0m(3PCAP) man page for details.  Reading a saved packet file doesn't require special privileges.
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       Using the SIGUSR2 signal along with the [1m-w[0m flag will forcibly flush the packet buffer into the output file.
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       use it) and will continue capturing packets. On platforms that do not support the SIGINFO signal, the same can be achieved by using the SIGUSR1 signal.
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       by typing your ``status'' character, typically control-T, although on some platforms, such as macOS, the ``status'' character is not set by default, so you must set it with [1mstty[0m(1) in order to
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       On platforms that support the SIGINFO signal, such as most BSDs (including macOS) and Digital/Tru64 UNIX, it will report those counts when it receives a SIGINFO signal (generated, for example,
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              the OS reports that information to applications; if not, it will be reported as 0).
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              packets ``dropped by kernel'' (this is the number of packets that were dropped, due to a lack of buffer space, by the packet capture mechanism in the OS on which [4mtcpdump[24m is running, if
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              yet, and on other OSes it counts only packets that were matched by the filter expression and were processed by [4mtcpdump[24m);
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              whether [4mtcpdump[24m has read and processed them yet, on other OSes it counts only packets that were matched by the filter expression regardless of whether [4mtcpdump[24m has read and processed them
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              command line, on some OSes it counts packets regardless of whether they were matched by the filter expression and, even if they were matched by the filter expression, regardless of
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              packets ``received by filter'' (the meaning of this depends on the OS on which you're running [4mtcpdump[24m, and possibly on the way the OS was configured - if a filter was specified on the
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM              packets ``captured'' (this is the number of packets that [4mtcpdump[24m has received and processed);
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       When [4mtcpdump[24m finishes capturing packets, it will report counts of:
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       number of packets have been processed.
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       C) or a SIGTERM signal (typically generated with the [1mkill[0m(1) command); if run with the [1m-c[0m flag, it will capture packets until it is interrupted by a SIGINT or SIGTERM signal or the specified
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       [4mTcpdump[24m will, if not run with the [1m-c[0m flag, continue capturing packets until it is interrupted by a SIGINT signal (generated, for example, by typing your interrupt character, typically control-
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       files. In all cases, only packets that match [4mexpression[24m will be processed by [4mtcpdump[24m.
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       which causes it to read from a saved packet file rather than to read packets from a network interface.  It can also be run with the [1m-V[0m flag, which causes it to read a list of saved packet
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       minutes, seconds, and fractions of a second since midnight.  It can also be run with the [1m-w[0m flag, which causes it to save the packet data to a file for later analysis, and/or with the [1m-r[0m flag,
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       [4mTcpdump[24m prints out a description of the contents of packets on a network interface that match the Boolean [4mexpression[24m; the description is preceded by a time stamp, printed, by default, as hours,
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM[1mDESCRIPTION[0m
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM               [ [4mexpression[24m ]
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM               [ [1m--micro[0m ] [ [1m--nano[0m ]
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM               [ [1m--time-stamp-precision=[0m[4mtstamp_precision[24m ]
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM               [ [1m-z[0m [4mpostrotate-command[24m ] [ [1m-Z[0m [4muser[24m ]
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM               [ [1m-V[0m [4mfile[24m ] [ [1m-w[0m [4mfile[24m ] [ [1m-W[0m [4mfilecount[24m ] [ [1m-y[0m [4mdatalinktype[24m ]
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM               [ [1m-r[0m [4mfile[24m ] [ [1m-s[0m [4msnaplen[24m ] [ [1m-T[0m [4mtype[24m ] [ [1m--version[0m ]
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM               [ [1m-Q[0m [4mpacket-metadata-filter[24m ] [ [1m-Q[0m [4min|out|inout[24m ]
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              savefile will have the name specified with the [1m-w[0m flag, with a number after it, starting at 1 and continuing upward.  The units of [4mfile_size[24m are millions of bytes (1,000,000 bytes, not
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              1,048,576 bytes).
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-d[0m     Dump the compiled packet-matching code in a human readable form to standard output and stop.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Please mind that although code compilation is always DLT-specific, typically it is impossible (and unnecessary) to specify which DLT to use for the dump because [4mtcpdump[24m uses either the
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              DLT of the input pcap file specified with [1m-r[0m, or the default DLT of the network interface specified with [1m-i[0m, or the particular DLT of the network interface specified with [1m-y[0m and [1m-i[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              respectively. In these cases the dump shows the same exact code that would filter the input file or the network interface without [1m-d[0m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              However, when neither [1m-r[0m nor [1m-i[0m is specified, specifying [1m-d[0m prevents [4mtcpdump[24m from guessing a suitable network interface (see [1m-i[0m).  In this case the DLT defaults to EN10MB and can be set
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              to another valid value manually with [1m-y[0m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-dd[0m    Dump packet-matching code as a [1mC[0m program fragment.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-ddd[0m   Dump packet-matching code as decimal numbers (preceded with a count).
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-D[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--list-interfaces[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Print the list of the network interfaces available on the system and on which [4mtcpdump[24m can capture packets.  For each network interface, a number and an interface name, possibly followed
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              by a text description of the interface, are printed.  The interface name or the number can be supplied to the [1m-i[0m flag to specify an interface on which to capture.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              This can be useful on systems that don't have a command to list them (e.g., Windows systems, or UNIX systems lacking [1mifconfig[0m [1m-a[0m); the number can be useful on Windows 2000 and later
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              systems, where the interface name is a somewhat complex string.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              The [1m-D[0m flag will not be supported if [4mtcpdump[24m was built with an older version of [4mlibpcap[24m that lacks the [1mpcap_findalldevs(3PCAP)[0m function.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-e[0m     Print the link-level header on each dump line.  This can be used, for example, to print MAC layer addresses for protocols such as Ethernet and IEEE 802.11.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-E[0m     Use [4mspi@ipaddr[24m [4malgo:secret[24m for decrypting IPsec ESP packets that are addressed to [4maddr[24m and contain Security Parameter Index value [4mspi[24m. This combination may be repeated with comma or
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              newline separation.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Note that setting the secret for IPv4 ESP packets is supported at this time.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Algorithms may be [1mdes-cbc[0m, [1m3des-cbc[0m, [1mblowfish-cbc[0m, [1mrc3-cbc[0m, [1mcast128-cbc[0m, or [1mnone[0m.  The default is [1mdes-cbc[0m.  The ability to decrypt packets is only present if [4mtcpdump[24m was compiled with
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              cryptography enabled.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              [4msecret[24m is the ASCII text for ESP secret key.  If preceded by 0x, then a hex value will be read.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       Reading packets from a network interface may require that you have special privileges; see the [1mpcap[0m(3PCAP) man page for details.  Reading a saved packet file doesn't require special privileges.
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       Using the SIGUSR2 signal along with the [1m-w[0m flag will forcibly flush the packet buffer into the output file.
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KAA[K[HM       use it) and will continue capturing packets. On platforms that do not support the SIGINFO signal, the same can be achieved by using the SIGUSR1 signal.
[63;1H[K:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Algorithms may be [1mdes-cbc[0m, [1m3des-cbc[0m, [1mblowfish-cbc[0m, [1mrc3-cbc[0m, [1mcast128-cbc[0m, or [1mnone[0m.  The default is [1mdes-cbc[0m.  The ability to decrypt packets is only present if [4mtcpdump[24m was compiled with
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              cryptography enabled.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              [4msecret[24m is the ASCII text for ESP secret key.  If preceded by 0x, then a hex value will be read.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              The option assumes RFC2406 ESP, not RFC1827 ESP.  The option is only for debugging purposes, and the use of this option with a true `secret' key is discouraged.  By presenting IPsec
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              secret key onto command line you make it visible to others, via [4mps[24m(1) and other occasions.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              In addition to the above syntax, the syntax [4mfile[24m [4mname[24m may be used to have tcpdump read the provided file in. The file is opened upon receiving the first ESP packet, so any special
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              permissions that tcpdump may have been given should already have been given up.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-f[0m     Print `foreign' IPv4 addresses numerically rather than symbolically (this option is intended to get around serious brain damage in Sun's NIS server — usually it hangs forever translating
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              non-local internet numbers).
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              The test for `foreign' IPv4 addresses is done using the IPv4 address and netmask of the interface on which capture is being done.  If that address or netmask are not available,
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              available, either because the interface on which capture is being done has no address or netmask or because the capture is being done on the Linux "any" interface, which can capture on
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              more than one interface, this option will not work correctly.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-F[0m [4mfile[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Use [4mfile[24m as input for the filter expression.  An additional expression given on the command line is ignored.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-g[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--apple-oneline[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Do not insert line break after IP header in verbose mode for easier parsing. This is an Apple addition.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-G[0m [4mrotate_seconds[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If specified, rotates the dump file specified with the [1m-w[0m option every [4mrotate_seconds[24m seconds.  Savefiles will have the name specified by [1m-w[0m which should include a time format as defined
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              by [1mstrftime[0m(3).  If no time format is specified, each new file will overwrite the previous.  Whenever a generated filename is not unique, tcpdump will overwrite the pre-existing data;
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              providing a time specification that is coarser than the capture period is therefore not advised.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If used in conjunction with the [1m-C[0m option, filenames will take the form of `[4mfile[24m<count>'.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-h[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--help[0m Print the tcpdump and libpcap version strings, print a usage message, and exit.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--version[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Print the tcpdump and libpcap version strings and exit.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-H[0m     Attempt to detect 802.11s draft mesh headers.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-i[0m [4minterface[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--interface=[0m[4minterface[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Listen, report the list of link-layer types, report the list of time stamp types, or report the results of compiling a filter expression on [4minterface[24m.  If unspecified and if the [1m-d[0m flag
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              is not given, [4mtcpdump[24m searches the system interface list for the lowest numbered, configured up interface (excluding loopback), which may turn out to be, for example, ``eth0''.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              On Linux systems with 2.2 or later kernels, an [4minterface[24m argument of ``any'' can be used to capture packets from all interfaces.  Note that captures on the ``any'' device will not be
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              done in promiscuous mode.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              On Darwin systems version 13 or later, when the [4minterface[24m is unspecified, tcpdump will use a pseudo interface to capture packets on a set of interfaces determined by the kernel (excludes
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              by default loopback and tunnel interfaces).
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Alternatively, to capture on more than one interface at a time, one may use "pktap" as the [4minterface[24m parameter followed by an optional list of comma separated interface names to include.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              For example, to capture on the loopback and en0 interface:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K                     [1mtcpdump[0m [1m-i[0m [1mpktap,lo0,en0[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              An [4minterface[24m argument of "all" or "pktap,all" can be used to capture packets from all interfaces, including loopback and tunnel interfaces.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              A pktap pseudo interface provides for packet metadata using the default PKTAP data link type and files are written in the Pcap-ng file format.  The RAW data link type must be used to
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              force to use the legacy [1mpcap-savefile[0m(5) file format with a ptkap pseudo interface.  Note that captures on a ptkap pseudo interface will not be done in promiscuous mode.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              An [4minterface[24m argument of "iptap" can be used to capture packets from at the IP layer.  This capture packets as they are passed to the input and output routines of the IPv4 and IPv6
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              protocol handlers of the networking stack.  Note that captures will not be done in promiscuous mode.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If the [1m-D[0m flag is supported, an interface number as printed by that flag can be used as the [4minterface[24m argument, if no interface on the system has that number as a name.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-I[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--monitor-mode[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Put the interface in "monitor mode"; this is supported only on IEEE 802.11 Wi-Fi interfaces, and supported only on some operating systems.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Note that in monitor mode the adapter might disassociate from the network with which it's associated, so that you will not be able to use any wireless networks with that adapter.  This
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              could prevent accessing files on a network server, or resolving host names or network addresses, if you are capturing in monitor mode and are not connected to another network with
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              another adapter.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              This flag will affect the output of the [1m-L[0m flag.  If [1m-I[0m isn't specified, only those link-layer types available when not in monitor mode will be shown; if [1m-I[0m is specified, only those
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              link-layer types available when in monitor mode will be shown.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--immediate-mode[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Capture in "immediate mode".  In this mode, packets are delivered to tcpdump as soon as they arrive, rather than being buffered for efficiency.  This is the default when printing packets
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              rather than saving packets to a ``savefile'' if the packets are being printed to a terminal rather than to a file or pipe.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-j[0m [4mtstamp_type[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--time-stamp-type=[0m[4mtstamp_type[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Set the time stamp type for the capture to [4mtstamp_type[24m.  The names to use for the time stamp types are given in [1mpcap-tstamp[0m(7); not all the types listed there will necessarily be valid
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              for any given interface.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-J[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--list-time-stamp-types[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              List the supported time stamp types for the interface and exit.  If the time stamp type cannot be set for the interface, no time stamp types are listed.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--time-stamp-precision=[0m[4mtstamp_precision[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              When capturing, set the time stamp precision for the capture to [4mtstamp_precision[24m.  Note that availability of high precision time stamps (nanoseconds) and their actual accuracy is
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              platform and hardware dependent.  Also note that when writing captures made with nanosecond accuracy to a savefile, the time stamps are written with nanosecond resolution, and the file
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              is written with a different magic number, to indicate that the time stamps are in seconds and nanoseconds; not all programs that read pcap savefiles will be able to read those captures.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              When reading a savefile, convert time stamps to the precision specified by [4mtimestamp_precision[24m, and display them with that resolution.  If the precision specified is less than the
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              precision of time stamps in the file, the conversion will lose precision.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              The supported values for [4mtimestamp_precision[24m are [1mmicro[0m for microsecond resolution and [1mnano[0m for nanosecond resolution.  The default is microsecond resolution.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--micro[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--nano[0m Shorthands for [1m--time-stamp-precision=micro[0m or [1m--time-stamp-precision=nano[0m, adjusting the time stamp precision accordingly.  When reading packets from a savefile, using [1m--micro[0m truncates
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              time stamps if the savefile was created with nanosecond precision.  In contrast, a savefile created with microsecond precision will have trailing zeroes added to the time stamp when
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              [1m--nano[0m is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-k[0m [4mmetadata_arg[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--apple-md-print[0m [4mmetadata_arg[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Control the display of packet metadata via an optional [4mmetadata_arg[24m argument. This is useful when displaying packet saved in the pcap-ng file format or with interfaces that support the
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              PKTAP data link type.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              By default, when the [4mmetadata_arg[24m optional argument is not specified, any available packet metadata information is printed out.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              The [4mmetadata_arg[24m argument controls the display of specific packet metadata information using a flag word, where each character corresponds to a type of packet metadata as follows:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K                     [1mI[0m     interface name (or interface ID)
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K                     [1mN[0m     process name
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K                     [1mP[0m     process ID
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K                     [1mS[0m     service class
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K                     [1mD[0m     direction
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K                     [1mC[0m     comment
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K                     [1mF[0m     flags
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K                     [1mU[0m     process UUID (not shown by default)
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K                     [1mV[0m     verbose printf of pcap-ng blocks (not shown by default)
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K                     [1md[0m     data link type
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K                     [1mf[0m     flow identifier
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K                     [1mt[0m     trace tag
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K                     [1mA[0m     display all types of metadata
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              This is an Apple modification.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-K[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--dont-verify-checksums[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Don't attempt to verify IP, TCP, or UDP checksums.  This is useful for interfaces that perform some or all of those checksum calculation in hardware; otherwise, all outgoing TCP
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              checksums will be flagged as bad.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              The option also suppresses truncated bytes missing warnings for ip and ip6 (Apple modification).
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-l[0m     Make stdout line buffered.  Useful if you want to see the data while capturing it.  E.g.,
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K                     [1mtcpdump[0m [1m-l[0m [1m|[0m [1mtee[0m [1mdat[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              or
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K                     [1mtcpdump[0m [1m-l[0m [1m>[0m [1mdat[0m [1m&[0m [1mtail[0m [1m-f[0m [1mdat[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Note that on Windows,``line buffered'' means ``unbuffered'', so that WinDump will write each character individually if [1m-l[0m is specified.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              [1m-U[0m is similar to [1m-l[0m in its behavior, but it will cause output to be ``packet-buffered'', so that the output is written to stdout at the end of each packet rather than at the end of each
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              line; this is buffered on all platforms, including Windows.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-L[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--list-data-link-types[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              List the known data link types for the interface, in the specified mode, and exit.  The list of known data link types may be dependent on the specified mode; for example, on some
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              platforms, a Wi-Fi interface might support one set of data link types when not in monitor mode (for example, it might support only fake Ethernet headers, or might support 802.11 headers
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              but not support 802.11 headers with radio information) and another set of data link types when in monitor mode (for example, it might support 802.11 headers, or 802.11 headers with radio
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              information, only in monitor mode).
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-m[0m [4mmodule[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Load SMI MIB module definitions from file [4mmodule[24m.  This option can be used several times to load several MIB modules into [4mtcpdump[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-M[0m [4msecret[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Use [4msecret[24m as a shared secret for validating the digests found in TCP segments with the TCP-MD5 option (RFC 2385), if present.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-n[0m     Don't convert addresses (i.e., host addresses, port numbers, etc.) to names.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-N[0m     Don't print domain name qualification of host names.  E.g., if you give this flag then [4mtcpdump[24m will print ``nic'' instead of ``nic.ddn.mil''.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-#[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--number[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Print an optional packet number at the beginning of the line.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Using the option twice prints the packet number from the beginning of file, also counting packets that have been filtered-out.  This requires the [1m-r[0m or [1m-V[0m options. This is an Apple
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              addition.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-O[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--no-optimize[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Do not run the packet-matching code optimizer.  This is useful only if you suspect a bug in the optimizer.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-P[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--apple-pcapng[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Use the pcap-ng file format when saving files.  This is an Apple addition.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-p[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--no-promiscuous-mode[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              [4mDon't[24m put the interface into promiscuous mode.  Note that the interface might be in promiscuous mode for some other reason; hence, `-p' cannot be used as an abbreviation for `ether host
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              {local-hw-addr} or ether broadcast'.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--print[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Print parsed packet output, even if the raw packets are being saved to a file with the [1m-w[0m flag.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-Q[0m [4mdirection[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--direction=[0m[4mdirection[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Choose send/receive direction [4mdirection[24m for which packets should be captured. Possible values are `in', `out' and `inout'. Not available on all platforms.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-Q[0m [4mmeta-data-expression[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--apple-md-filter[0m [4mmeta-data-expression[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See the PACKET METADATA FILTER section below.  This is an Apple addition.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-q[0m     Quick (quiet?) output.  Print less protocol information so output lines are shorter.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-r[0m [4mfile[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Read packets from [4mfile[24m (which was created with the [1m-w[0m option or by other tools that write pcap or pcapng files).  Standard input is used if [4mfile[24m is ``-''.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-S[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--absolute-tcp-sequence-numbers[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Print absolute, rather than relative, TCP sequence numbers.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-s[0m [4msnaplen[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--snapshot-length=[0m[4msnaplen[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Snarf [4msnaplen[24m bytes of data from each packet rather than the default of 262144 bytes.  Packets truncated because of a limited snapshot are indicated in the output with ``[|[4mproto[24m]'',
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              where [4mproto[24m is the name of the protocol level at which the truncation has occurred.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Note that taking larger snapshots both increases the amount of time it takes to process packets and, effectively, decreases the amount of packet buffering.  This may cause packets to be
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              lost.  Note also that taking smaller snapshots will discard data from protocols above the transport layer, which loses information that may be important.  NFS and AFS requests and
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              replies, for example, are very large, and much of the detail won't be available if a too-short snapshot length is selected.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If you need to reduce the snapshot size below the default, you should limit [4msnaplen[24m to the smallest number that will capture the protocol information you're interested in.  Setting
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              [4msnaplen[24m to 0 sets it to the default of 262144, for backwards compatibility with recent older versions of [4mtcpdump[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-T[0m [4mtype[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Force packets selected by "[4mexpression[24m" to be interpreted the specified [4mtype[24m.  Currently known types are [1maodv[0m (Ad-hoc On-demand Distance Vector protocol), [1mcarp[0m (Common Address Redundancy
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Protocol), [1mcnfp[0m (Cisco NetFlow protocol), [1mdomain[0m (Domain Name System), [1mlmp[0m (Link Management Protocol), [1mpgm[0m (Pragmatic General Multicast), [1mpgm_zmtp1[0m (ZMTP/1.0 inside PGM/EPGM), [1mptp[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (Precision Time Protocol), [1mradius[0m (RADIUS), [1mresp[0m (REdis Serialization Protocol), [1mrpc[0m (Remote Procedure Call), [1mrtcp[0m (Real-Time Applications control protocol), [1mrtp[0m (Real-Time Applications
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              protocol), [1msnmp[0m (Simple Network Management Protocol), [1msomeip[0m (SOME/IP), [1mtftp[0m (Trivial File Transfer Protocol), [1mvat[0m (Visual Audio Tool), [1mvxlan[0m (Virtual eXtensible Local Area Network), [1mwb[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (distributed White Board) and [1mzmtp1[0m (ZeroMQ Message Transport Protocol 1.0).
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Note that the [1mpgm[0m type above affects UDP interpretation only, the native PGM is always recognised as IP protocol 113 regardless. UDP-encapsulated PGM is often called "EPGM" or "PGM/UDP".
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Note that the [1mpgm_zmtp1[0m type above affects interpretation of both native PGM and UDP at once. During the native PGM decoding the application data of an ODATA/RDATA packet would be
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              decoded as a ZeroMQ datagram with ZMTP/1.0 frames.  During the UDP decoding in addition to that any UDP packet would be treated as an encapsulated PGM packet.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Additional dissectors for non registered UDP protocols: [1miperf[0m (iperf 2.x), [1miperf3[0m (iperf 3.x), [1miperf3-64[0m (iperf 3.x with 64 bits packet ID), [1msuttp[0m (Simple UDP Throughput Test Protocol),
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-t[0m     [4mDon't[24m print a timestamp on each dump line.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-tt[0m    Print the timestamp, as seconds since January 1, 1970, 00:00:00, UTC, and fractions of a second since that time, on each dump line.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-ttt[0m   Print a delta (microsecond or nanosecond resolution depending on the [1m--time-stamp-precision[0m option) between current and previous line on each dump line.  The default is microsecond
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              resolution.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-tttt[0m  Print a timestamp, as hours, minutes, seconds, and fractions of a second since midnight, preceded by the date, on each dump line.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-ttttt[0m Print a delta (microsecond or nanosecond resolution depending on the [1m--time-stamp-precision[0m option) between current and first line on each dump line.  The default is microsecond
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              resolution.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-t[0m [1mn[0m   An alternate form for specifying the kind of timestamp display where [1mn[0m is a number between 0 and 5 with the following meaning:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K                     [1m0[0m     time
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K                     [1m1[0m     no time
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K                     [1m2[0m     unformatted timestamp
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K                     [1m3[0m     microseconds since previous line
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K                     [1m4[0m     date and time
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K                     [1m5[0m     microseconds since first line
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              This option may be specified more than once to display more than one kind of timestamp on each dump line.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-u[0m     Print undecoded NFS handles.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-U[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--packet-buffered[0m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If the [1m-w[0m option is not specified, or if it is specified but the [1m--print[0m flag is also specified, make the printed packet output ``packet-buffered''; i.e., as the description of the
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              contents of each packet is printed, it will be written to the standard output, rather than, when not writing to a terminal, being written only when the output buffer fills.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If the [1m-w[0m option is specified, make the saved raw packet output ``packet-buffered''; i.e., as each packet is saved, it will be written to the output file, rather than being written only
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              when the output buffer fills.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              The [1m-U[0m flag will not be supported if [4mtcpdump[24m was built with an older version of [4mlibpcap[24m that lacks the [1mpcap_dump_flush(3PCAP)[0m function.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-v[0m     When parsing and printing, produce (slightly more) verbose output.  For example, the time to live, identification, total length and options in an IP packet are printed.  Also enables
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              additional packet integrity checks such as verifying the IP and ICMP header checksum.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              When writing to a file with the [1m-w[0m option and at the same time not reading from a file with the [1m-r[0m option, report to stderr, once per second, the number of packets captured. In Solaris,
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              FreeBSD and possibly other operating systems this periodic update currently can cause loss of captured packets on their way from the kernel to tcpdump.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              On Darwin systems when writing to a file with the [1m-w[0m option, the number of packets captured is not updated if there as been no new packets in the last second.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-vv[0m    Even more verbose output.  For example, additional fields are printed from NFS reply packets, and SMB packets are fully decoded.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-vvv[0m   Even more verbose output.  For example, telnet [1mSB[0m ... [1mSE[0m options are printed in full.  With [1m-X[0m Telnet options are printed in hex as well.
[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-V[0m [4mfile[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Read a list of filenames from [4mfile[24m. Standard input is used if [4mfile[24m is ``-''.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-w[0m [4mfile[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Write the raw packets to [4mfile[24m rather than parsing and printing them out.  They can later be printed with the -r option.  Standard output is used if [4mfile[24m is ``-''.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              This output will be buffered if written to a file or pipe, so a program reading from the file or pipe may not see packets for an arbitrary amount of time after they are received.  Use
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              the [1m-U[0m flag to cause packets to be written as soon as they are received.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              The MIME type [4mapplication/vnd.tcpdump.pcap[24m has been registered with IANA for [4mpcap[24m files. The filename extension [4m.pcap[24m appears to be the most commonly used along with [4m.cap[24m and [4m.dmp[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              [4mTcpdump[24m itself doesn't check the extension when reading capture files and doesn't add an extension when writing them (it uses magic numbers in the file header instead). However, many
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              operating systems and applications will use the extension if it is present and adding one (e.g. .pcap) is recommended.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See [1mpcap-savefile[0m(5) for a description of the file format.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-W[0m [4mfilecount[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Used in conjunction with the [1m-C[0m option, this will limit the number of files created to the specified number, and begin overwriting files from the beginning, thus creating a 'rotating'
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              buffer.  In addition, it will name the files with enough leading 0s to support the maximum number of files, allowing them to sort correctly.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Used in conjunction with the [1m-G[0m option, this will limit the number of rotated dump files that get created, exiting with status 0 when reaching the limit.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If used in conjunction with both [1m-C[0m and [1m-G,[0m the [1m-W[0m option will currently be ignored, and will only affect the file name.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-x[0m     When parsing and printing, in addition to printing the headers of each packet, print the data of each packet (minus its link level header) in hex.  The smaller of the entire packet or
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              [4msnaplen[24m bytes will be printed.  Note that this is the entire link-layer packet, so for link layers that pad (e.g. Ethernet), the padding bytes will also be printed when the higher layer
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              packet is shorter than the required padding.  In the current implementation this flag may have the same effect as [1m-xx[0m if the packet is truncated.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-xx[0m    When parsing and printing, in addition to printing the headers of each packet, print the data of each packet, [4mincluding[24m its link level header, in hex.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-X[0m     When parsing and printing, in addition to printing the headers of each packet, print the data of each packet (minus its link level header) in hex and ASCII.  This is very handy for
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              analysing new protocols.  In the current implementation this flag may have the same effect as [1m-XX[0m if the packet is truncated.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-XX[0m    When parsing and printing, in addition to printing the headers of each packet, print the data of each packet, [4mincluding[24m its link level header, in hex and ASCII.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m-y[0m [4mdatalinktype[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       [1m--linktype=[0m[4mdatalinktype[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Set the data link type to use while capturing packets (see [1m-L[0m) or just compiling and dumping packet-matching code (see [1m-d[0m) to [4mdatalinktype[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
