:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              This option is global and does not need to be specified for each use of --next.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --libcurl is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --libcurl client.c https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m-v,[24m [4m--verbose[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --limit-rate <speed>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Specify the maximum transfer rate you want curl to use - for both downloads and uploads. This feature is useful if you have a limited pipe and you would like your transfer not to use
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              your entire bandwidth. To make it slower than it otherwise would be.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              The given speed is measured in bytes/second, unless a suffix is appended.  Appending 'k' or 'K' counts the number as kilobytes, 'm' or 'M' makes it megabytes, while 'g' or 'G' makes it
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              gigabytes. The suffixes (k, M, G, T, P) are 1024 based. For example 1k is 1024. Examples: 200K, 3m and 1G.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              The rate limiting logic works on averaging the transfer speed to no more than the set threshold over a period of multiple seconds.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If you also use the [4m-Y,[24m [4m--speed-limit[24m option, that option takes precedence and might cripple the rate-limiting slightly, to help keeping the speed-limit logic working.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --limit-rate is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Examples:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --limit-rate 100K https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --limit-rate 1000 https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --limit-rate 10M https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--rate[24m, [4m-Y,[24m [4m--speed-limit[24m and [4m-y,[24m [4m--speed-time[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       -l, --list-only
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (FTP POP3 SFTP) (FTP) When listing an FTP directory, this switch forces a name-only view. This is especially useful if the user wants to machine-parse the contents of an FTP directory
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              since the normal directory view does not use a standard look or format. When used like this, the option causes an NLST command to be sent to the server instead of LIST.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Note: Some FTP servers list only files in their response to NLST; they do not include sub-directories and symbolic links.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (SFTP) When listing an SFTP directory, this switch forces a name-only view, one per line.  This is especially useful if the user wants to machine-parse the contents of an SFTP directory
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              since the normal directory view provides more information than just file names.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (POP3) When retrieving a specific email from POP3, this switch forces a LIST command to be performed instead of RETR. This is particularly useful if the user wants to see if a specific
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              message-id exists on the server and what size it is.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Note: When combined with [4m-X,[24m [4m--request[24m, this option can be used to send a UIDL command instead, so the user may use the email's unique identifier rather than its message-id to make the
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              request.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Providing --list-only multiple times has no extra effect.  Disable it again with --no-list-only.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --list-only ftp://example.com/dir/
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m-Q,[24m [4m--quote[24m and [4m-X,[24m [4m--request[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --local-port <num/range>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Set a preferred single number or range (FROM-TO) of local port numbers to use for the connection(s). Note that port numbers by nature are a scarce resource so setting this range to
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              something too narrow might cause unnecessary connection setup failures.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --local-port is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --local-port 1000-3000 https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m-g,[24m [4m--globoff[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --location-trusted
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (HTTP) Like [4m-L,[24m [4m--location[24m, but allows sending the name + password to all hosts that the site may redirect to. This may or may not introduce a security breach if the site redirects you
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              to a site to which you send your authentication info (which is clear-text in the case of HTTP Basic authentication).
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Providing --location-trusted multiple times has no extra effect.  Disable it again with --no-location-trusted.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --location-trusted -u user:password https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m-u,[24m [4m--user[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       -L, --location
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (HTTP) If the server reports that the requested page has moved to a different location (indicated with a Location: header and a 3XX response code), this option makes curl redo the
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              request on the new place. If used together with [4m-i,[24m [4m--include[24m or [4m-I,[24m [4m--head[24m, headers from all requested pages are shown.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              When authentication is used, curl only sends its credentials to the initial host. If a redirect takes curl to a different host, it does not get the user+password pass on. See also
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              [4m--location-trusted[24m on how to change this.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Limit the amount of redirects to follow by using the [4m--max-redirs[24m option.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              When curl follows a redirect and if the request is a POST, it sends the following request with a GET if the HTTP response was 301, 302, or 303. If the response code was any other 3xx
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              code, curl resends the following request using the same unmodified method.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              You can tell curl to not change POST requests to GET after a 30x response by using the dedicated options for that: [4m--post301[24m, [4m--post302[24m and [4m--post303[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              The method set with [4m-X,[24m [4m--request[24m overrides the method curl would otherwise select to use.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Providing --location multiple times has no extra effect.  Disable it again with --no-location.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl -L https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--resolve[24m and [4m--alt-svc[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --login-options <options>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (IMAP LDAP POP3 SMTP) Specify the login options to use during server authentication.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              You can use login options to specify protocol specific options that may be used during authentication. At present only IMAP, POP3 and SMTP support login options. For more information
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              about login options please see RFC 2384, RFC 5092 and the IETF draft https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-earhart-url-smtp-00
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Since 8.2.0, IMAP supports the login option "AUTH=+LOGIN". With this option, curl uses the plain (not SASL) "LOGIN IMAP" command even if the server advertises SASL authentication. Care
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              should be taken in using this option, as it sends your password over the network in plain text. This does not work if the IMAP server disables the plain "LOGIN" (e.g. to prevent password
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              snooping).
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --login-options is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --login-options 'AUTH=*' imap://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m-u,[24m [4m--user[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --mail-auth <address>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (SMTP) Specify a single address. This is used to specify the authentication address (identity) of a submitted message that is being relayed to another server.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --mail-auth is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --mail-auth user@example.come -T mail smtp://example.com/
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--mail-rcpt[24m and [4m--mail-from[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --mail-from <address>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (SMTP) Specify a single address that the given mail should get sent from.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --mail-from is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --mail-from user@example.com -T mail smtp://example.com/
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--mail-rcpt[24m and [4m--mail-auth[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --mail-rcpt-allowfails
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (SMTP) When sending data to multiple recipients, by default curl aborts SMTP conversation if at least one of the recipients causes RCPT TO command to return an error.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              The default behavior can be changed by passing [4m--mail-rcpt-allowfails[24m command-line option which makes curl ignore errors and proceed with the remaining valid recipients.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If all recipients trigger RCPT TO failures and this flag is specified, curl still aborts the SMTP conversation and returns the error received from to the last RCPT TO command.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Providing --mail-rcpt-allowfails multiple times has no extra effect.  Disable it again with --no-mail-rcpt-allowfails.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --mail-rcpt-allowfails --mail-rcpt dest@example.com smtp://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--mail-rcpt[24m. Added in 7.69.0.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --mail-rcpt <address>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (SMTP) Specify a single email address, user name or mailing list name. Repeat this option several times to send to multiple recipients.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              When performing an address verification ([1mVRFY[0m command), the recipient should be specified as the user name or user name and domain (as per Section 3.5 of RFC 5321).
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              When performing a mailing list expand (EXPN command), the recipient should be specified using the mailing list name, such as "Friends" or "London-Office".
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              --mail-rcpt can be used several times in a command line
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --mail-rcpt user@example.net smtp://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--mail-rcpt-allowfails[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       -M, --manual
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Manual. Display the huge help text.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --manual
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m-v,[24m [4m--verbose[24m, [4m--libcurl[24m and [4m--trace[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --max-filesize <bytes>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (FTP HTTP MQTT) Specify the maximum size (in bytes) of a file to download. If the file requested is larger than this value, the transfer does not start and curl returns with exit code
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              63.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              A size modifier may be used. For example, Appending 'k' or 'K' counts the number as kilobytes, 'm' or 'M' makes it megabytes, while 'g' or 'G' makes it gigabytes. Examples: 200K, 3m and
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              1G. (Added in 7.58.0)
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              [1mNOTE[0m: before curl 8.4.0, when the file size is not known prior to download, for such files this option has no effect even if the file transfer ends up being larger than this given limit.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Starting with curl 8.4.0, this option aborts the transfer if it reaches the threshold during transfer.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --max-filesize is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --max-filesize 100K https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--limit-rate[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --max-redirs <num>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (HTTP) Set maximum number of redirections to follow. When [4m-L,[24m [4m--location[24m is used, to prevent curl from following too many redirects, by default, the limit is set to 50 redirects. Set
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              this option to -1 to make it unlimited.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --max-redirs is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --max-redirs 3 --location https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m-L,[24m [4m--location[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       -m, --max-time <fractional seconds>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Maximum time in seconds that you allow each transfer to take. This is useful for preventing your batch jobs from hanging for hours due to slow networks or links going down. This option
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              accepts decimal values.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If you enable retrying the transfer ([4m--retry[24m) then the maximum time counter is reset each time the transfer is retried. You can use [4m--retry-max-time[24m to limit the retry time.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              The decimal value needs to provided using a dot (.) as decimal separator - not the local version even if it might be using another separator.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --max-time is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Examples:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --max-time 10 https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --max-time 2.92 https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--connect-timeout[24m and [4m--retry-max-time[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --metalink
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              This option was previously used to specify a Metalink resource. Metalink support is disabled in curl for security reasons (added in 7.78.0).
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --metalink is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --metalink file https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m-Z,[24m [4m--parallel[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --negotiate
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (HTTP) Enables Negotiate (SPNEGO) authentication.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              This option requires a library built with GSS-API or SSPI support. Use [4m-V,[24m [4m--version[24m to see if your curl supports GSS-API/SSPI or SPNEGO.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              When using this option, you must also provide a fake [4m-u,[24m [4m--user[24m option to activate the authentication code properly. Sending a '-u :' is enough as the user name and password from the [4m-u,[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              [4m--user[24m option are not actually used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Providing --negotiate multiple times has no extra effect.
[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --negotiate -u : https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--basic[24m, [4m--ntlm[24m, [4m--anyauth[24m and [4m--proxy-negotiate[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --netrc-file <filename>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              This option is similar to [4m-n,[24m [4m--netrc[24m, except that you provide the path (absolute or relative) to the netrc file that curl should use. You can only specify one netrc file per invocation.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              It abides by [4m--netrc-optional[24m if specified.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --netrc-file is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --netrc-file netrc https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m-n,[24m [4m--netrc[24m, [4m-u,[24m [4m--user[24m and [4m-K,[24m [4m--config[24m. This option is mutually exclusive to [4m-n,[24m [4m--netrc[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --netrc-optional
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Similar to [4m-n,[24m [4m--netrc[24m, but this option makes the .netrc usage [1moptional[0m and not mandatory as the [4m-n,[24m [4m--netrc[24m option does.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Providing --netrc-optional multiple times has no extra effect.  Disable it again with --no-netrc-optional.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --netrc-optional https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--netrc-file[24m. This option is mutually exclusive to [4m-n,[24m [4m--netrc[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       -n, --netrc
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Makes curl scan the [4m.netrc[24m file in the user's home directory for login name and password. This is typically used for FTP on Unix. If used with HTTP, curl enables user authentication. See
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              [4mnetrc(5)[24m and [4mftp(1)[24m for details on the file format. Curl does not complain if that file does not have the right permissions (it should be neither world- nor group-readable). The
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              environment variable "HOME" is used to find the home directory.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              On Windows two filenames in the home directory are checked: [4m.netrc[24m and [4m_netrc[24m, preferring the former. Older versions on Windows checked for [4m_netrc[24m only.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              A quick and simple example of how to setup a [4m.netrc[24m to allow curl to FTP to the machine host.domain.com with user name 'myself' and password 'secret' could look similar to:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              machine host.domain.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              login myself
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              password secret
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Providing --netrc multiple times has no extra effect.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Disable it again with --no-netrc.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --netrc https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--netrc-file[24m, [4m-K,[24m [4m--config[24m and [4m-u,[24m [4m--user[24m. This option is mutually exclusive to [4m--netrc-file[24m and [4m--netrc-optional[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       -:, --next
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Tells curl to use a separate operation for the following URL and associated options. This allows you to send several URL requests, each with their own specific options, for example, such
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              as different user names or custom requests for each.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              [4m-:,[24m [4m--next[24m resets all local options and only global ones have their values survive over to the operation following the [4m-:,[24m [4m--next[24m instruction. Global options include [4m-v,[24m [4m--verbose[24m,
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              [4m--trace[24m, [4m--trace-ascii[24m and [4m--fail-early[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              For example, you can do both a GET and a POST in a single command line:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              curl www1.example.com --next -d postthis www2.example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              --next can be used several times in a command line
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Examples:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl https://example.com --next -d postthis www2.example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl -I https://example.com --next https://example.net/
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m-Z,[24m [4m--parallel[24m and [4m-K,[24m [4m--config[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --no-alpn
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (HTTPS) Disable the ALPN TLS extension. ALPN is enabled by default if libcurl was built with an SSL library that supports ALPN. ALPN is used by a libcurl that supports HTTP/2 to
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              negotiate HTTP/2 support with the server during https sessions.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Note that this is the negated option name documented. You can use --alpn to enable ALPN.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Providing --no-alpn multiple times has no extra effect.  Disable it again with --alpn.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --no-alpn https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--no-npn[24m and [4m--http2[24m. [4m--no-alpn[24m requires that the underlying libcurl was built to support TLS.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       -N, --no-buffer
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Disables the buffering of the output stream. In normal work situations, curl uses a standard buffered output stream that has the effect that it outputs the data in chunks, not
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              necessarily exactly when the data arrives. Using this option disables that buffering.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Note that this is the negated option name documented. You can use --buffer to enable buffering again.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Providing --no-buffer multiple times has no extra effect.  Disable it again with --buffer.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --no-buffer https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m-#,[24m [4m--progress-bar[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --no-clobber
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              When used in conjunction with the [4m-o,[24m [4m--output[24m, [4m-J,[24m [4m--remote-header-name[24m, [4m-O,[24m [4m--remote-name[24m, or [4m--remote-name-all[24m options, curl avoids overwriting files that already exist. Instead, a
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              dot and a number gets appended to the name of the file that would be created, up to filename.100 after which it does not create any file.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Note that this is the negated option name documented. You can thus use --clobber to enforce the clobbering, even if [4m-J,[24m [4m--remote-header-name[24m is specified.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Providing --no-clobber multiple times has no extra effect.  Disable it again with --clobber.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --no-clobber --output local/dir/file https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m-o,[24m [4m--output[24m and [4m-O,[24m [4m--remote-name[24m. Added in 7.83.0.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --no-keepalive
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Disables the use of keepalive messages on the TCP connection. curl otherwise enables them by default.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Note that this is the negated option name documented. You can thus use --keepalive to enforce keepalive.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Providing --no-keepalive multiple times has no extra effect.  Disable it again with --keepalive.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --no-keepalive https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--keepalive-time[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --no-npn
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (HTTPS) curl never uses NPN, this option has no effect (added in 7.86.0).
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Disable the NPN TLS extension. NPN is enabled by default if libcurl was built with an SSL library that supports NPN. NPN is used by a libcurl that supports HTTP/2 to negotiate HTTP/2
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              support with the server during https sessions.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Providing --no-npn multiple times has no extra effect.  Disable it again with --npn.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --no-npn https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--no-alpn[24m and [4m--http2[24m. [4m--no-npn[24m requires that the underlying libcurl was built to support TLS.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --no-progress-meter
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Option to switch off the progress meter output without muting or otherwise affecting warning and informational messages like [4m-s,[24m [4m--silent[24m does.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Note that this is the negated option name documented. You can thus use --progress-meter to enable the progress meter again.
[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Providing --no-progress-meter multiple times has no extra effect.  Disable it again with --progress-meter.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --no-progress-meter -o store https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m-v,[24m [4m--verbose[24m and [4m-s,[24m [4m--silent[24m. Added in 7.67.0.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --no-sessionid
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (TLS) Disable curl's use of SSL session-ID caching. By default all transfers are done using the cache. Note that while nothing should ever get hurt by attempting to reuse SSL
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              session-IDs, there seem to be broken SSL implementations in the wild that may require you to disable this in order for you to succeed.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Note that this is the negated option name documented. You can thus use --sessionid to enforce session-ID caching.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Providing --no-sessionid multiple times has no extra effect.  Disable it again with --sessionid.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --no-sessionid https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m-k,[24m [4m--insecure[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --noproxy <no-proxy-list>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Comma-separated list of hosts for which not to use a proxy, if one is specified. The only wildcard is a single "*" character, which matches all hosts, and effectively disables the proxy.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Each name in this list is matched as either a domain which contains the hostname, or the hostname itself. For example, "local.com" would match "local.com", "local.com:80", and
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              "www.local.com", but not "www.notlocal.com".
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              This option overrides the environment variables that disable the proxy ("no_proxy" and "NO_PROXY") (added in 7.53.0). If there is an environment variable disabling a proxy, you can set
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              the no proxy list to "" to override it.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              IP addresses specified to this option can be provided using CIDR notation (added in 7.86.0): an appended slash and number specifies the number of network bits out of the address to use
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              in the comparison. For example "192.168.0.0/16" would match all addresses starting with "192.168".
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --noproxy is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --noproxy "www.example" https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m-x,[24m [4m--proxy[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --ntlm-wb
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (HTTP) Enables NTLM much in the style [4m--ntlm[24m does, but hand over the authentication to the separate binary "ntlmauth" application that is executed when needed.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Providing --ntlm-wb multiple times has no extra effect.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --ntlm-wb -u user:password https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--ntlm[24m and [4m--proxy-ntlm[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --ntlm (HTTP) Enables NTLM authentication. The NTLM authentication method was designed by Microsoft and is used by IIS web servers. It is a proprietary protocol, reverse-engineered by clever
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              people and implemented in curl based on their efforts. This kind of behavior should not be endorsed, you should encourage everyone who uses NTLM to switch to a public and documented
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              authentication method instead, such as Digest.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If you want to enable NTLM for your proxy authentication, then use [4m--proxy-ntlm[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Providing --ntlm multiple times has no extra effect.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --ntlm -u user:password https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--proxy-ntlm[24m. [4m--ntlm[24m requires that the underlying libcurl was built to support TLS. This option is mutually exclusive to [4m--basic[24m and [4m--negotiate[24m and [4m--digest[24m and [4m--anyauth[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --oauth2-bearer <token>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (IMAP LDAP POP3 SMTP HTTP) Specify the Bearer Token for OAUTH 2.0 server authentication. The Bearer Token is used in conjunction with the user name which can be specified as part of the
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              [4m--url[24m or [4m-u,[24m [4m--user[24m options.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              The Bearer Token and user name are formatted according to RFC 6750.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --oauth2-bearer is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --oauth2-bearer "mF_9.B5f-4.1JqM" https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--basic[24m, [4m--ntlm[24m and [4m--digest[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --output-dir <dir>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              This option specifies the directory in which files should be stored, when [4m-O,[24m [4m--remote-name[24m or [4m-o,[24m [4m--output[24m are used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              The given output directory is used for all URLs and output options on the command line, up until the first [4m-:,[24m [4m--next[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If the specified target directory does not exist, the operation fails unless [4m--create-dirs[24m is also used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --output-dir is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --output-dir "tmp" -O https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m-O,[24m [4m--remote-name[24m and [4m-J,[24m [4m--remote-header-name[24m. Added in 7.73.0.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       -o, --output <file>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Write output to <file> instead of stdout. If you are using {} or [] to fetch multiple documents, you should quote the URL and you can use '#' followed by a number in the <file>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              specifier. That variable is replaced with the current string for the URL being fetched. Like in:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              curl "http://{one,two}.example.com" -o "file_#1.txt"
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              or use several variables like:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              curl "http://{site,host}.host[1-5].example" -o "#1_#2"
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              You may use this option as many times as the number of URLs you have. For example, if you specify two URLs on the same command line, you can use it like this:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              curl -o aa example.com -o bb example.net
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              and the order of the -o options and the URLs does not matter, just that the first -o is for the first URL and so on, so the above command line can also be written as
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              curl example.com example.net -o aa -o bb
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also the [4m--create-dirs[24m option to create the local directories dynamically. Specifying the output as '-' (a single dash) passes the output to stdout.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              To suppress response bodies, you can redirect output to /dev/null:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              curl example.com -o /dev/null
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Or for Windows:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              curl example.com -o nul
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              --output can be used several times in a command line
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Examples:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl -o file https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl "http://{one,two}.example.com" -o "file_#1.txt"
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl "http://{site,host}.host[1-5].example" -o "#1_#2"
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl -o file https://example.com -o file2 https://example.net
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m-O,[24m [4m--remote-name[24m, [4m--remote-name-all[24m and [4m-J,[24m [4m--remote-header-name[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --parallel-immediate
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              When doing parallel transfers, this option instructs curl that it should rather prefer opening up more connections in parallel at once rather than waiting to see if new transfers can be
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              added as multiplexed streams on another connection.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              This option is global and does not need to be specified for each use of --next.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Providing --parallel-immediate multiple times has no extra effect.  Disable it again with --no-parallel-immediate.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --parallel-immediate -Z https://example.com -o file1 https://example.com -o file2
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m-Z,[24m [4m--parallel[24m and [4m--parallel-max[24m. Added in 7.68.0.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --parallel-max <num>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              When asked to do parallel transfers, using [4m-Z,[24m [4m--parallel[24m, this option controls the maximum amount of transfers to do simultaneously.
[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              This option is global and does not need to be specified for each use of [4m-:,[24m [4m--next[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              The default is 50.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --parallel-max is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --parallel-max 100 -Z https://example.com ftp://example.com/
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m-Z,[24m [4m--parallel[24m. Added in 7.66.0.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       -Z, --parallel
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Makes curl perform its transfers in parallel as compared to the regular serial manner.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              This option is global and does not need to be specified for each use of --next.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Providing --parallel multiple times has no extra effect.  Disable it again with --no-parallel.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --parallel https://example.com -o file1 https://example.com -o file2
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m-:,[24m [4m--next[24m and [4m-v,[24m [4m--verbose[24m. Added in 7.66.0.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --pass <phrase>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (SSH TLS) Passphrase for the private key.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --pass is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --pass secret --key file https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--key[24m and [4m-u,[24m [4m--user[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --path-as-is
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Tell curl to not handle sequences of /../ or /./ in the given URL path. Normally curl squashes or merges them according to standards but with this option set you tell it not to do that.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Providing --path-as-is multiple times has no extra effect.  Disable it again with --no-path-as-is.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --path-as-is https://example.com/../../etc/passwd
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--request-target[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --pinnedpubkey <hashes>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (TLS) Tells curl to use the specified public key file (or hashes) to verify the peer. This can be a path to a file which contains a single public key in PEM or DER format, or any number
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              of base64 encoded sha256 hashes preceded by 'sha256//' and separated by ';'.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              When negotiating a TLS or SSL connection, the server sends a certificate indicating its identity. A public key is extracted from this certificate and if it does not exactly match the
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              public key provided to this option, curl aborts the connection before sending or receiving any data.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              This option is independent of option [4m-k,[24m [4m--insecure[24m. If you use both options together then the peer is still verified by public key.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              PEM/DER support:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              OpenSSL and GnuTLS, wolfSSL (added in 7.43.0), mbedTLS , Secure Transport macOS 10.7+/iOS 10+ (7.54.1), Schannel (7.58.1)
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              sha256 support:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              OpenSSL, GnuTLS and wolfSSL, mbedTLS (added in 7.47.0), Secure Transport macOS 10.7+/iOS 10+ (7.54.1), Schannel (7.58.1)
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Other SSL backends not supported.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --pinnedpubkey is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Examples:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --pinnedpubkey keyfile https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --pinnedpubkey 'sha256//ce118b51897f4452dc' https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--hostpubsha256[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --post301
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (HTTP) Tells curl to respect RFC 7231/6.4.2 and not convert POST requests into GET requests when following a 301 redirection. The non-RFC behavior is ubiquitous in web browsers, so curl
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              does the conversion by default to maintain consistency. However, a server may require a POST to remain a POST after such a redirection. This option is meaningful only when using [4m-L,[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              [4m--location[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Providing --post301 multiple times has no extra effect.  Disable it again with --no-post301.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --post301 --location -d "data" https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--post302[24m, [4m--post303[24m and [4m-L,[24m [4m--location[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --post302
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (HTTP) Tells curl to respect RFC 7231/6.4.3 and not convert POST requests into GET requests when following a 302 redirection. The non-RFC behavior is ubiquitous in web browsers, so curl
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              does the conversion by default to maintain consistency. However, a server may require a POST to remain a POST after such a redirection. This option is meaningful only when using [4m-L,[24m
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              [4m--location[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Providing --post302 multiple times has no extra effect.  Disable it again with --no-post302.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --post302 --location -d "data" https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--post301[24m, [4m--post303[24m and [4m-L,[24m [4m--location[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --post303
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (HTTP) Tells curl to violate RFC 7231/6.4.4 and not convert POST requests into GET requests when following 303 redirections. A server may require a POST to remain a POST after a 303
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              redirection. This option is meaningful only when using [4m-L,[24m [4m--location[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Providing --post303 multiple times has no extra effect.  Disable it again with --no-post303.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --post303 --location -d "data" https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--post302[24m, [4m--post301[24m and [4m-L,[24m [4m--location[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --preproxy [protocol://]host[:port]
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Use the specified SOCKS proxy before connecting to an HTTP or HTTPS [4m-x,[24m [4m--proxy[24m. In such a case curl first connects to the SOCKS proxy and then connects (through SOCKS) to the HTTP or
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              HTTPS proxy. Hence pre proxy.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              The pre proxy string should be specified with a protocol:// prefix to specify alternative proxy protocols. Use socks4://, socks4a://, socks5:// or socks5h:// to request the specific
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              SOCKS version to be used. No protocol specified makes curl default to SOCKS4.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If the port number is not specified in the proxy string, it is assumed to be 1080.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              User and password that might be provided in the proxy string are URL decoded by curl. This allows you to pass in special characters such as @ by using %40 or pass in a colon with %3a.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --preproxy is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --preproxy socks5://proxy.example -x http://http.example https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m-x,[24m [4m--proxy[24m and [4m--socks5[24m. Added in 7.52.0.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       -#, --progress-bar
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Make curl display transfer progress as a simple progress bar instead of the standard, more informational, meter.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              This progress bar draws a single line of '#' characters across the screen and shows a percentage if the transfer size is known. For transfers without a known size, there is a space ship
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (-=o=-) that moves back and forth but only while data is being transferred, with a set of flying hash sign symbols on top.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              This option is global and does not need to be specified for each use of --next.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Providing --progress-bar multiple times has no extra effect.  Disable it again with --no-progress-bar.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl -# -O https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--styled-output[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --proto-default <protocol>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Tells curl to use [4mprotocol[24m for any URL missing a scheme name.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              An unknown or unsupported protocol causes error [4mCURLE_UNSUPPORTED_PROTOCOL[24m (1).
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              This option does not change the default proxy protocol (http).
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Without this option set, curl guesses protocol based on the host name, see [4m--url[24m for details.
[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --proto-default is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --proto-default https ftp.example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--proto[24m and [4m--proto-redir[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --proto-redir <protocols>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Tells curl to limit what protocols it may use on redirect. Protocols denied by [4m--proto[24m are not overridden by this option. See [4m--proto[24m for how protocols are represented.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example, allow only HTTP and HTTPS on redirect:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              curl --proto-redir -all,http,https http://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              By default curl only allows HTTP, HTTPS, FTP and FTPS on redirects (added in 7.65.2). Specifying [4mall[24m or [4m+all[24m enables all protocols on redirects, which is not good for security.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --proto-redir is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --proto-redir =http,https https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--proto[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --proto <protocols>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Tells curl to limit what protocols it may use for transfers. Protocols are evaluated left to right, are comma separated, and are each a protocol name or 'all', optionally prefixed by
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              zero or more modifiers. Available modifiers are:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              +      Permit this protocol in addition to protocols already permitted (this is the default if no modifier is used).
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              -      Deny this protocol, removing it from the list of protocols already permitted.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              =      Permit only this protocol (ignoring the list already permitted), though subject to later modification by subsequent entries in the comma separated list.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              For example: [4m--proto[24m -ftps uses the default protocols, but disables ftps
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              [4m--proto[24m -all,https,+http only enables http and https
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              [4m--proto[24m =http,https also only enables http and https
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Unknown and disabled protocols produce a warning. This allows scripts to safely rely on being able to disable potentially dangerous protocols, without relying upon support for that
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              protocol being built into curl to avoid an error.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              This option can be used multiple times, in which case the effect is the same as concatenating the protocols into one instance of the option.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --proto is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --proto =http,https,sftp https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--proto-redir[24m and [4m--proto-default[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --proxy-anyauth
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Tells curl to pick a suitable authentication method when communicating with the given HTTP proxy. This might cause an extra request/response round-trip.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Providing --proxy-anyauth multiple times has no extra effect.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --proxy-anyauth --proxy-user user:passwd -x proxy https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m-x,[24m [4m--proxy[24m, [4m--proxy-basic[24m and [4m--proxy-digest[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --proxy-basic
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Tells curl to use HTTP Basic authentication when communicating with the given proxy. Use [4m--basic[24m for enabling HTTP Basic with a remote host. Basic is the default authentication method
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              curl uses with proxies.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Providing --proxy-basic multiple times has no extra effect.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --proxy-basic --proxy-user user:passwd -x proxy https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m-x,[24m [4m--proxy[24m, [4m--proxy-anyauth[24m and [4m--proxy-digest[24m.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --proxy-ca-native
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              (TLS) Tells curl to use the CA store from the native operating system to verify the HTTPS proxy. By default, curl uses a CA store provided in a single file or directory, but when using
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              this option it interfaces the operating system's own vault.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              This option works for curl on Windows when built to use OpenSSL, wolfSSL (added in 8.3.0) or GnuTLS (added in 8.5.0). When curl on Windows is built to use Schannel, this feature is
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              implied and curl then only uses the native CA store.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Providing --proxy-ca-native multiple times has no extra effect.  Disable it again with --no-proxy-ca-native.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --ca-native https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--cacert[24m, [4m--capath[24m and [4m-k,[24m [4m--insecure[24m. Added in 8.2.0.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --proxy-cacert <file>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Same as [4m--cacert[24m but used in HTTPS proxy context.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --proxy-cacert is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --proxy-cacert CA-file.txt -x https://proxy https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--proxy-capath[24m, [4m--cacert[24m, [4m--capath[24m and [4m-x,[24m [4m--proxy[24m. Added in 7.52.0.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --proxy-capath <dir>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Same as [4m--capath[24m but used in HTTPS proxy context.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --proxy-capath is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --proxy-capath /local/directory -x https://proxy https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--proxy-cacert[24m, [4m-x,[24m [4m--proxy[24m and [4m--capath[24m. Added in 7.52.0.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --proxy-cert-type <type>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Same as [4m--cert-type[24m but used in HTTPS proxy context.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --proxy-cert-type is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --proxy-cert-type PEM --proxy-cert file -x https://proxy https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--proxy-cert[24m. Added in 7.52.0.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --proxy-cert <cert[:passwd]>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Same as [4m-E,[24m [4m--cert[24m but used in HTTPS proxy context.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --proxy-cert is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --proxy-cert file -x https://proxy https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--proxy-cert-type[24m. Added in 7.52.0.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --proxy-ciphers <list>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Same as [4m--ciphers[24m but used in HTTPS proxy context.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Specifies which ciphers to use in the connection to the HTTPS proxy. The list of ciphers must specify valid ciphers. Read up on SSL cipher list details on this URL:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              https://curl.se/docs/ssl-ciphers.html
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --proxy-ciphers is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Example:
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K               curl --proxy-ciphers ECDHE-ECDSA-AES256-CCM8 -x https://proxy https://example.com
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              See also [4m--ciphers[24m, [4m--curves[24m and [4m-x,[24m [4m--proxy[24m. Added in 7.52.0.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K       --proxy-crlfile <file>
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              Same as [4m--crlfile[24m but used in HTTPS proxy context.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K              If --proxy-crlfile is provided several times, the last set value is used.
:[K[K [KESCESC[KOO[KBB[K
